preamble: The phonetic transformation from a voiced alveolar fricative /z/ to a voiceless alveolar plosive /t/ involves two distinct but often co-occurring phonetic processes: devoicing and fortition. Devoicing is the loss of vocal fold vibration, rendering the sound voiceless. Fortition, or strengthening, refers to an increase in the degree of obstruction in the vocal tract, changing a fricative (produced with partial obstruction and turbulent airflow) into a plosive (produced with complete obstruction and a sudden release). This shift is less common than lenition (weakening) but is attested in specific historical linguistic contexts and language contact situations.

examples.note: Adjustment of word-final /z/ to /t/ in loanwords from Luganda due to syllable structure constraints (NOCODA constraint).
A characteristic phonological innovation in the Tekela group of Nguni languages (including Swati), where /t/ corresponds to /z/ in the Zunda group (e.g., Zulu, Xhosa).

sources: A Phonological Analysis of Borrowed Nouns from Luganda to Kupsabiny. (2025).
van der Spuy, A. (1990). Phonological relationships between the Southern Bantu languages.
Doke, C. M. (1954). The Southern Bantu Languages. Oxford University Press.
Msimang, C. T. (1989). A Comparative Phonological Study of the Nguni Languages. University of South Africa.
